md: If multiplying powers **adds** exponents, dividing them should **subtract**. And it does, for a reason you can watch happen: dividing is cancelling, and cancelling removes blocks in pairs.

blocks.title: The claim
blocks.body: Dividing powers of the same base subtracts the exponents:  $\;\dfrac{a^m}{a^n} = a^{m-n}.$

md: Draw $a^m$ on top and $a^n$ on the bottom. A factor of $a$ on top and a factor of $a$ on the bottom cancel to $1$, because $\frac{a}{a} = 1$. Slide $m$ and $n$ and watch matching blocks cancel.
Every block on the bottom cancels one block on the top. If the top started with $m$ blocks and $n$ of them get cancelled, then $m - n$ blocks survive:

blocks.title: What if you run out of blocks?
blocks.body: So far we assumed $m > n$, so blocks are left on top. But what about $a^3 / a^3$, where everything cancels? Or $a^2 / a^5$, where the bottom runs out last? The subtraction still works, it just hands you $a^0$ or a negative exponent, and those have meanings of their own. That is the next proof: [[zero-and-negative-exponents]].
